- The Health and Behavioural Impacts of COVID-19 on Youth: Results from the Canadian Perspectives Survey Series 1
- Canadians' mental health during the COVID-19 pandemic (crowdsourcing)
- Indigenous people and mental health during the COVID-19 pandemic (crowdsourcing)
Public opinion polls on alcohol and cannabis consumption behaviours during the COVID-19 pandemic
The Canadian Centre for Substance Use and Addiction (CCSA), working with NANOS Research, conducted a public opinion poll on alcohol and cannabis consumption among people in Canada during the COVID-19 pandemic as well as on feelings of isolation and anxiety. Findings from the poll were released in April 2020 and prompted discussion about substance use during the pandemic, providing guidance on how to reduce risk when using substances during the crisis. See: https://www.ccsa.ca/sites/default/files/2020-04/CCSA-NANOS-Alcohol-Consumption-During-COVID-19-Report-2020-en.pdf .
CCSA conducted a follow-up poll focussing on patterns of alcohol use during COVID-19 and factors contributing to changes to patterns of use such as stress, boredom and a lack of a regular schedule. The results were publicly released in June 2020: https://www.ccsa.ca/sites/default/files/2020-06/CCSA-NANOS-Increased-Alcohol-Consumption-During-COVID-19-Report-2020-en_0.pdf .

#### Research related to COVID-19, mental health, and substance use
In April 2020, the Canadian Institutes of Health Research (CIHR), in collaboration with Health Canada and PHAC, launched the COVID-19 and Mental Health (CMH) Initiative to provide timely evidence to decision makers on mental health and substance use responses in the context of COVID-19.
Rapid knowledge syntheses
CIHR and partners launched an initial research funding competition in early April 2020 to support rapid knowledge syntheses and mobilization of current evidence on mental health and substance use. Through this competition, CIHR and partners are supporting 45 research teams across the country in providing policymakers at the municipal, provincial, territorial and federal levels with timely, high-quality evidence needed to improve mental health outcomes during the pandemic response. This includes support for: 1) a rapid research review of the effects of epidemics or pandemics on suicide, and thoughts and behaviours related to suicide ; 2) a review of available evidence on conducting school-based suicide risk assessments with youth via eHealth ; and 3) a review of programs that support mental wellness for Indigenous children and youth in rural and remote communities . Preliminary results of the 45 funded research syntheses are available online on CIHR's website .
Supporting implementation science and population-level intervention research
In June 2020, CIHR and partners announced further funding to support implementation science and population-level intervention research to address the impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic and its containment measures on mental health and substance use. Research applicants were encouraged to consider populations that may be experiencing acute mental health concerns associated with COVID-19, including healthcare professionals and front-line workers directly engaged in responding to COVID-19. In total, CIHR and its partners have invested $10.3 million to support 55 research projects. This funding is supporting projects on mitigating suicide risk during the COVID-19 pandemic via telehealth and tracking the prevalence and incidence of modifiable suicide risk factors during the COVID-19 pandemic . Interim and final reports will be made available online. These efforts will help inform future interventions and where possible, the scaling of effective programs across Canada. This will inform evaluation plans and results indicators for programming as we move into COVID-19 recovery and consider scaling up effective programs for the longer term.
Research on problematic substance use
As highlighted by the WHO, problematic substance use is associated with increased risk of suicide. Footnote 53 Given this link, the following research related to COVID-19 is of relevance to suicide prevention.
CIHR provided funding to the Canadian Research Initiative in Substance Misuse (CRISM) to undertake urgent activities to support people who use drugs, decision-makers and care providers in light of COVID-19. These activities included the creation of six national guidance documents and a rapid assessment of the challenges faced by people who use drugs during the COVID-19 pandemic. CRISM's national guidance documents have been available online since July 2020:
- Supporting people who use substances in shelter settings during the COVID-19 pandemic
- Telemedicine support for addiction services
- Supporting people who use substances in acute care settings during the COVID-19 pandemic
- Medications and other clinical approaches to support physical distancing for people who use substances during the COVID-19 pandemic
- Strategies to reduce SARS-CoV-2 transmission in supportive recovery programs and residential addiction treatment services
- Harm reduction worker safety during the COVID-19 global pandemic .

#### Evidence review on interventions for alcohol-related suicide
PHAC is supporting an evidence review on interventions that can address alcohol-related suicide, focusing on applying this to the COVID-19 context. This review will summarize original research over the past 30 years. It will also result in a narrative review of policies and programs to reduce alcohol-related suicide, including how these interventions may be applied in the context of the global pandemic.

### Appendix D: PHAC programs supporting mental health promotion among specific groups
PHAC mental health promotion programs act at individual, family, community and systems levels to address health inequities, reduce risk factors, and increase protective factors for mental health. These programs are helping to build knowledge and evidence about the effectiveness of metal health promotion interventions, how they work, for whom, and in what context. While there is evidence that mental health promotion interventions can improve protective factors such as resilience, additional research is necessary to understand the role of these types of upstream actions on suicide prevention.

#### Mental Health Promotion Innovation Fund
The Mental Health Promotion Innovation Fund (MHP-IF) is a national program designed to test interventions and build evidence on results related to mental health outcomes. It funds population health interventions that promote protective factors and address the underlying determinants of mental health and health equity for children, youth, young adults and their caregivers. The MHP-IF projects' focus is on the design, development, delivery and testing of promising and innovative mental health promotion interventions. Projects operate across multiple sites and jurisdictions to engage participants from a variety of communities including First Nations, Inuit, Métis, newcomer and immigrant, and LGBTQ2 groups.
In particular, the MHP-IF project Qaujigiartiit Health Research Centre: Inuusirvik Community Wellness Hub – Pilot of a holistic, community-centred wellness program model for Inuit families in Nunavut, builds on the existing pathways to wellness to improve mental health by coordinating existing programs into an integrated community-led mental health and wellness service delivery model. This includes land-based programs, literacy programs, early childhood education programs, Inuit counselling services, suicide prevention, and maternal-child health programs. While the project has just begun, progress and results will be shared through the MHP-IF Knowledge Development and Exchange (KDE) Hub, an interactive web-based platform to share lessons learned, connect with various stakeholders and apply new knowledge to a broader policy and system change agenda.

#### Promoting health equity: Mental Health of Black Canadians initiative
In September 2020, PHAC released a knowledge product titled “ Social determinants and inequities in health for Black Canadians: A Snapshot ”, which highlights how anti-Black racism and systemic discrimination are key drivers of health inequalities faced by diverse Black communities in Canada.
Projects funded by PHAC's Promoting Health Equity: Mental Health of Black Canadians initiative , launched in October 2018, address mental health promotion and the social determinants of mental health, such as anti-Black racism (internalized, interpersonal, institutional / systemic), education, policing and criminal justice systems, poverty, social exclusion, and/or stigma and discrimination.
